section.title: Elihu's Second Speech
section.sectionRationale: Elihu turns from addressing Job personally to defending God's justice as a general principle, answering Job's charge that God has denied him justice. He argues that a God who governs the whole earth impartially, showing no favoritism to princes over paupers, cannot possibly act wickedly, and he grows increasingly harsh toward Job, accusing him of speaking without knowledge and adding rebellion to his suffering.
section.thesis: Elihu rightly insists that the Judge of all the earth cannot act unjustly, since injustice would undo the very order that holds the world together, but he wrongly assumes this settles the specific question of Job's own suffering, and his closing harshness shows he, too, can overreach.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Elihu's summons to the wise to judge Job's words; his declaration that God, far from God to do wrong, governs with perfect justice; his description of God's impartial sovereignty over kings and commoners alike; and a hypothetical scene of repentance followed by Elihu's harsh verdict that Job has added rebellion to his sin.

verses.text: Then Elihu continued:
“Hear my words, O wise men; give ear to me, O men of learning.
For the ear tests words as the mouth tastes food.
Let us choose for ourselves what is right; let us learn together what is good.
For Job has declared, ‘I am righteous, yet God has deprived me of justice.
Would I lie about my case? My wound is incurable, though I am without transgression.’
What man is like Job, who drinks up derision like water?
He keeps company with evildoers and walks with wicked men.
For he has said, ‘It profits a man nothing that he should delight in God.’

units.label: Far be it from God to do wrong
units.note: He repays according to a man's deeds

verses.text: Therefore listen to me, O men of understanding. Far be it from God to do wrong, and from the Almighty to act {{rasha|wickedly}}.
For according to a man’s deeds He repays him; according to a man’s ways He brings consequences.
Indeed, it is true that God does not act wickedly, and the Almighty does not pervert {{mishpat|justice}}.
Who gave Him charge over the earth? Who appointed Him over the whole world?
If He were to set His heart to it and withdraw His Spirit and breath,
all flesh would perish together and mankind would return to the dust.
If you have understanding, hear this; listen to my words.
Could one who hates justice govern? Will you condemn the just and mighty One,
who says to kings, ‘You are worthless!’ and to nobles, ‘You are wicked,’
who is not {{panim|partial}} to princes and does not favor rich over poor? For they are all the work of His hands.
They die in an instant, in the middle of the night. The people convulse and pass away; the mighty are removed without human hand.
For His eyes are on the ways of a man, and He sees his every step.
There is no darkness or deep shadow where the workers of iniquity can hide.
For God need not examine a man further or have him approach for judgment.
He shatters the mighty without inquiry and sets up others in their place.
Therefore, He recognizes their deeds; He overthrows them in the night and they are crushed.
He strikes them for their wickedness in full view,
because they turned aside from Him and had no regard for any of His ways.
They caused the cry of the poor to come before Him, and He heard the outcry of the afflicted.
But when He remains silent, who can condemn Him? When He hides His face, who can see Him? Yet He watches over both man and nation,
that godless men should not rule or lay snares for the people.
Suppose someone says to God, ‘I have endured my punishment; I will offend no more.
Teach me what I cannot see; if I have done wrong, I will not do it again.’
Should God repay you on your own terms when you have disavowed His? You must choose, not I; so tell me what you know.
Men of understanding will declare to me, and the wise men who hear me will say:
‘Job speaks without knowledge; his words lack insight.’
If only Job were tried to the utmost for answering like a wicked man.
For he adds {{pesha|rebellion}} to his sin; he claps his hands among us and multiplies his words against God.”

rasha.gloss: To act wickedly, to do wrong. Elihu insists it is unthinkable, 'far be it,' that God would act this way, an important true premise that he then applies with more confidence than the situation warrants.

mishpat.gloss: Justice, judgment, the right ordering of a case. Elihu argues God cannot pervert mishpat, since He governs the whole earth; the question the book leaves open is whether this truth explains Job's specific suffering or simply restates a premise Job never actually denied.

panim.gloss: Literally 'to lift the face,' an idiom for showing partiality or favoritism. Elihu insists God is not partial to princes over paupers, since all are equally 'the work of His hands,' a genuinely important biblical truth about God's impartial justice, Deuteronomy 10:17.

pesha.gloss: Rebellion, transgression, willful revolt, often distinguished from unintentional sin. Elihu's final accusation that Job 'adds rebellion to his sin' is his harshest charge yet, treating Job's protest of innocence as active defiance against God rather than honest lament.

basic.context: Elihu shifts from addressing Job personally to arguing a general theological point before an imagined audience of wise men: God cannot possibly be unjust, since He governs the whole world impartially, showing no favoritism between kings and commoners. He grows harsher as he goes, eventually accusing Job of adding rebellion to his suffering by continuing to protest his innocence.
basic.hermeneutics: Elihu's core claim, that God is not and cannot be unjust, is true and important; the flaw is in the application, assuming that Job's charge of being 'denied justice' must therefore be false pride rather than honest confusion from a man who genuinely does not understand why he is suffering. True theology applied without listening can still wound.
basic.originalLanguages: Elihu insists it is unthinkable that God would rasha, act wickedly, or pervert mishpat, justice, since He shows no nasa panim, partiality, between rich and poor. Yet his conclusion, that Job has added pesha, rebellion, to his suffering, shows Elihu himself overreaching into a harsh verdict the narrative does not actually support.
basic.typology: Elihu's insistence that God judges impartially, seeing every deed and hearing every cry of the oppressed, anticipates the New Testament's teaching on a final impartial judgment executed through Christ, who judges the world in righteousness, Acts 17:31.
basic.christ: Elihu rightly says God 'need not examine a man further' since His eyes are already on every step, a truth fully realized in Christ, who is entrusted with all judgment because the Father has given Him authority as the Son of Man, John 5:22, 27; every deed done in darkness will be exposed before Him.
basic.god: God's justice truly is impartial, as Elihu insists, not favoring rich over poor, king over commoner; yet the same book that contains Elihu's confident speech will soon show God speaking to Job not with a philosophical defense of His impartial justice but with an overwhelming display of His wisdom and power, redirecting the whole question.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 10:17 describes God as showing no partiality and accepting no bribe. Acts 17:31 announces a fixed day of righteous judgment through Christ. John 5:22, 27 gives Christ authority to judge. Romans 2:11 states plainly that God shows no favoritism.
basic.oneStory: Elihu's true claim that God judges impartially, seeing every hidden deed, anticipates the final judgment secured through Christ. Yet the chapter also shows how even true theology, spoken with too much certainty about someone else's pain, can slide into harsh judgment rather than help; only Christ's judgment is perfectly matched to perfect knowledge and perfect love.

context.literary: Elihu's second speech shifts audience, no longer addressing Job alone but calling on 'wise men' to judge between them, a rhetorical strategy that increases the pressure on Job by implying a wider jury agrees with Elihu's assessment.
context.historical: The ancient world commonly assumed that true kings and judges must be perfectly impartial; Elihu applies this ideal to God Himself, arguing that since even human justice, at its best, cannot favor the powerful over the weak, God's justice certainly cannot either.
context.canonical: Elihu's insistence on God's impartial judgment of both king and commoner echoes the Torah's own description of God in Deuteronomy 10:17-18 and anticipates the New Testament's teaching that all people, without exception, will stand before Christ's righteous judgment.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To defend God's justice as a matter of principle against Job's specific complaint of being denied justice, while unintentionally illustrating how confident theological argument, even when substantially true, can become harsh and premature when applied without full knowledge of another person's situation.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Elihu's core claim, that God cannot act unjustly and shows no partiality, is a prescriptive and enduring truth affirmed throughout Scripture. His specific application of it to conclude that Job's protests amount to rebellion is Elihu's own fallible judgment, not an endorsed conclusion of the book.

debates.question: Is Elihu's accusation in 34:37, that Job 'adds rebellion to his sin,' a fair reading of Job's earlier speeches?

views.label: A fair warning against genuine presumption
views.case: Job has, at points, spoken as though he could out argue God, 13:3, and God's own speech in 38-41 will gently rebuke Job's presumption in judging divine governance, suggesting Elihu is not entirely wrong to flag this danger.

views.label: An overreaching, harsh mischaracterization
views.case: God's epilogue verdict, 42:7-8, says Job, not the friends, spoke rightly about Him, which does not fit comfortably with Elihu's charge that Job's continued protest of innocence was itself rebellious; Elihu's harshness here seems to outrun the evidence, much like the three friends before him.

debates.bearing: Both elements can be true: Job does need the correction God will bring in chapters 38-41 about the limits of his own knowledge, yet Elihu's specific charge of 'rebellion' seems to go further than the text ultimately supports, since Job is not among those condemned in 42:7.

debates.question: Does Elihu's argument in 34:10-15 actually answer Job's specific complaint of being denied justice?

views.label: Yes, sufficiently
views.case: If God cannot act unjustly by definition, since injustice would undo His own governance of creation, then Job's charge must rest on a misunderstanding, not on any real fault in God.

views.label: No, it restates a premise without addressing the specific case
views.case: Job has never denied that God is just in general; his complaint is that he, specifically, cannot see how his own suffering fits any justice he can discern. A general defense of divine justice does not by itself explain a particular hard providence, which is why God's own speech takes a different approach entirely, appealing to wisdom and creation rather than to abstract justice.

debates.bearing: The book's own resolution, God's speech from the whirlwind, notably does not repeat Elihu's argument but instead redirects Job toward wonder and trust, suggesting Elihu's approach, while true, was not sufficient on its own.

study.originalLanguages: Elihu insists it would be unthinkable for God to rasha, act wickedly, or to pervert mishpat, justice, since He shows no nasa panim, partiality, favoring neither king nor commoner. His final charge, that Job has added pesha, willful rebellion, to his earlier sin, marks Elihu's harshest and most personally accusatory moment in either speech so far.

typology.type: God's impartial judgment, seeing every deed done even in darkness, 34:21-22
typology.fulfillment: The final, impartial judgment of all people entrusted to Christ, Acts 17:31, John 5:22, 27.

study.christ: Elihu's confidence that God's eyes are on every man's ways, that no darkness can hide the workers of iniquity, and that God shows no partiality between rich and poor, anticipates the New Testament's teaching that final judgment has been entrusted entirely to the Son, John 5:22, 27, who will judge the world in righteousness on a day God has fixed, Acts 17:31. What Elihu argues in principle here, God's perfectly just governance, finds its concrete, personal fulfillment in Christ's coming judgment.
study.god: God truly does govern with impartial justice, as Elihu insists, showing no favoritism between the powerful and the powerless, exactly as the Torah itself declares, Deuteronomy 10:17. Yet God's actual response to Job, when it comes in chapters 38-41, will not be an abstract defense of His own justice but an invitation into wonder at His wisdom, suggesting Elihu's true premise was not, by itself, the full answer Job needed.

crossReferences.note: Describes God as showing no partiality and executing justice for the fatherless and the widow, the same impartiality Elihu appeals to here.
States plainly that God shows no favoritism, a New Testament echo of Elihu's central claim.
The Father has given all judgment to the Son, fulfilling the impartial, all seeing judgment Elihu describes here.
God's verdict names only the three friends as having spoken wrongly, notably not repeating Elihu's harsh charge that Job had rebelled.

study.oneStory: Elihu rightly insists that the Judge of all the earth cannot be unjust and shows no partiality between rich and poor, a truth the whole Bible affirms and one day finally displays in Christ's perfectly righteous judgment. But his harsh application of this truth to Job, accusing him of rebellion, shows that even sound theology needs humility and patience to be spoken well, something only fully modeled in the ministry of Christ Himself.

sermon.title: Job: Rebuked in Suffering
sermon.why: Piper's sermon on Job 32:1-37:24 treats the whole of Elihu's speeches together, including this defense of God's impartial justice in chapter 34, framing Elihu's rebuke of Job's presumption as a preparation for God's own address to Job from the whirlwind.

quiz.q: What is Elihu's central claim in 34:10-15?

options.text: It is unthinkable for God to act wickedly or pervert justice, since He sustains and governs all creation.
options.note: Verses 10-12 state this directly, with verses 14-15 grounding it in God's sustaining power over all life.

options.text: God sometimes acts unjustly but should not be questioned about it.
options.note: Elihu's whole argument is that God cannot act unjustly at all, not that injustice is permissible but unquestionable.

options.text: Justice is a purely human concept that does not apply to God.
options.note: Elihu applies the concept of justice directly to God, arguing He perfectly embodies it, not that it is irrelevant to Him.

options.text: God's justice mainly works through the moral order built into creation, functioning almost automatically.
options.note: Close to Elihu's broader argument, but he specifically stresses God's direct, personal governance and attention, not merely an impersonal built-in order.

quiz.q: What does Elihu mean when he says God is not 'partial to princes,' 34:19?

options.text: God treats the powerful and the powerless with equal justice, since both are equally His creation.
options.note: Verse 19 grounds this impartiality in the fact that all people are 'the work of His hands.'

options.text: God favors kings and rulers over ordinary people.
options.note: Elihu says the opposite; God shows no such favoritism.

options.text: God has no interest in how nations are governed.
options.note: The surrounding verses show God actively removing unjust rulers, 34:24-25, showing real interest in governance.

options.text: God judges powerful rulers only when their wrongdoing becomes extremely severe.
options.note: Close, God does judge rulers, but Elihu's point is that God's justice is constant and impartial, not triggered only by extreme severity.

quiz.q: What is Elihu's final, harshest charge against Job in 34:36-37?

options.text: That Job has added rebellion to his sin, multiplying words against God without knowledge.
options.note: This is Elihu's most severe accusation in the speech, wishing Job would be 'tried to the utmost.'

options.text: That Job has never sinned at all.
options.note: Elihu's accusation is the opposite, that Job's continued protest constitutes active rebellion.

options.text: That Job's friends were entirely correct in their earlier speeches.
options.note: Elihu has separately criticized the three friends for failing to actually refute Job, 32:3.

options.text: That Job should simply stop speaking and quietly accept his suffering.
options.note: Close to the spirit of Elihu's frustration, but his actual charge is sharper and more specific: that Job's continued protest amounts to willful rebellion, not merely too much talking.

quiz.q: How does the epilogue in 42:7-8 relate to Elihu's charge that Job rebelled by speaking without knowledge?

options.text: God's verdict names only the three friends as having spoken wrongly, not repeating Elihu's specific charge against Job as rebellion.
options.note: This suggests Elihu's harsh conclusion here outran what the book itself ultimately affirms about Job.

options.text: God gently corrects Job's presumption in the whirlwind speeches, though without ever calling it rebellion the way Elihu does.
options.note: True and close, God does correct Job's presumption in chapters 38-41, but 42:7-8 specifically vindicates Job's speech over the friends', stronger than Elihu's harsh charge allows.

options.text: God condemns Elihu by name alongside the three friends.
options.note: Elihu is notably absent from the list of those God rebukes, which is itself significant, though the text does not explicitly praise him either.

options.text: The epilogue never mentions Job's earlier speeches at all.
options.note: 42:7-8 directly references what Job and the friends 'spoke' about God, engaging their speeches directly.

quiz.q: How does Elihu's picture of God's impartial, all seeing justice anticipate the New Testament?

options.text: It anticipates the final, impartial judgment of all people entrusted to Christ.
options.note: Acts 17:31 and John 5:22, 27 develop exactly this theme of impartial judgment given to the Son.

options.text: It teaches that judgment will never actually occur.
options.note: Elihu's whole point is that God's judgment is active and inescapable, a theme carried forward into final judgment.

options.text: It shows that only the wealthy will be judged by God.
options.note: Elihu's point is the opposite, that rich and poor are judged with equal impartiality.

options.text: It mainly reflects ancient legal ideals about kings, with only loose connection to New Testament teaching on judgment.
options.note: Partly true about the ancient background, but the impartial, all seeing judgment Elihu describes maps directly onto Acts 17:31 and John 5:22, 27, a real anticipation, not merely a loose connection.

questions: Elihu was right that God cannot be unjust, but he applied that truth harshly to a suffering man. Where might you need to hold a true belief with more gentleness toward someone who is hurting?
Elihu insists God shows no favoritism between rich and poor. Are there ways you unconsciously treat some people's concerns as more important to God than others?

Elihu accuses Job of rebellion for continuing to protest his innocence, yet God later says Job spoke rightly. What helps you tell the difference between honest lament and actual rebellion against God in your own life?
God's justice is impartial and all seeing, nothing done in darkness escapes Him. Does that truth comfort you or unsettle you more right now, and why?
Elihu's true theology, applied without enough humility, became harsh. Think of a time someone corrected you with truth but without gentleness. How did it land, and what would you have needed instead?

translationNotes.note: 'The ear tests words as the mouth tastes food' is a proverbial image for careful discernment, inviting Elihu's audience to weigh his argument rather than accept it uncritically.
'Without human hand' describes sudden deaths attributed directly to God's action rather than to any visible human agent, emphasizing His unmediated sovereignty over life and death.

'When He remains silent, who can condemn Him' captures Elihu's argument that God's inscrutability itself is proof no one is qualified to judge His ways, an idea that anticipates the tone, though not the specific content, of God's own speech in chapters 38-41.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 34:1-37. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
